Article.ArticleTitle: Influence of hemicelluloses on the formation of regenerated Camellia oleifera shells holocellulose films.

Abstract.AbstractText: Camellia oleifera shells (COS), a by-product of the tea-oil industry, are characterized by a high hemicellulose content and a relatively low cellulose level. Effective utilization of COS based on their unique composition can reduce agricultural waste and add value to the tea-oil industry. In this study, COS holocellulose (COSH) was converted into strong and biodegradable films through a process involving dissolution, gelation, and regeneration, using zinc chloride molten salt hydrate (ZnCl₂ MSH) as an environmentally friendly solvent. A NaOH pretreatment with varying concentrations (1-10 wt%) and durations (1-4 h) was applied to COSH to investigate the impact of hemicelluloses on the structure and properties of the resulting films. By applying a dilute (1-1.5 wt%) and short (1-2h) NaOH pretreatment, the degradation of both cellulose and hemicellulose during film formation was significantly reduced, leading to regenerated films with enhanced hydrophobicity, thermal and mechanical properties. Notably, films derived from COSH treated with 1.5 wt% NaOH for 2 h contained the highest amount of hemicellulose and exhibited the highest tensile strength (26.46 MPa) and Young's modulus (2158.95 MPa) among all samples. This work highlights the potential for fabricating biodegradable films from regenerated holocellulose.
